Summary: Configure Dinosaur entity with required fields
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system to store the basic identification data of each dinosaur, so that no specimen information is lost between system sessions.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The Dinosaur entity includes the following required fields: FirstName, LastName, Username, Email.
- AC2: The entity also supports optional fields: Age, Zone, Sector, Address, Phone, DietType, CreatedAt.
- AC3: The entity is mapped to a database table via EF Core.
- AC4: CreatedAt is automatically set on record creation.

Summary: Set up EF Core DbContext
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system to persist dinosaur data to a database, so that records are available across multiple sessions without data loss.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: A DbContext class is created and includes a DbSet<Dinosaur> property.
- AC2: The DbContext is configured to connect to a SQL Server database via connection string.
- AC3: The application registers the DbContext through dependency injection or direct instantiation.

Summary: Create and run initial database migration
Description:
As an investigator, I want the database schema to be created automatically from the entity model, so that the system is ready to store data without manual SQL scripts.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: Running Add-Migration InitialCreate generates a valid migration file.
- AC2: Running Update-Database applies the migration and creates the Dinosaurs table.
- AC3: The table structure matches the Dinosaur entity definition.

Summary: Enforce uniqueness on Username and Email
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system to prevent two dinosaurs from sharing the same username or registration code, so that each specimen can be uniquely identified and tracked.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The database enforces a unique constraint on the Username column.
- AC2: The database enforces a unique constraint on the Email column.
- AC3: Attempting to insert a duplicate username or email raises a detectable error.

Summary: Register a new dinosaur with required fields
Description:
As an investigator, I want to register a new dinosaur by providing its name, species, username, and registration code, so that the specimen becomes part of the official park record.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system prompts for FirstName, LastName, Username, and Email.
- AC2: All four required fields must be non-empty; the system rejects the registration otherwise.
- AC3: The Email field must follow a valid email format (contains @ and domain).
- AC4: On success, the system displays a confirmation message that the dinosaur was registered correctly.

Summary: Reject registration if username already exists
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system to prevent registering a dinosaur with a username that is already in use, so that each specimen has a unique identifier in the park.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: Before saving, the system checks whether the username already exists in the database.
- AC2: If the username exists, registration is rejected and an error message is displayed.
- AC3: No record is created in the database when the username is duplicate.

Summary: Reject registration if email already exists
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system to prevent registering a dinosaur with a registration code already assigned to another specimen, so that each lab code maps to exactly one dinosaur.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: Before saving, the system checks whether the email already exists in the database.
- AC2: If the email exists, registration is rejected and an error message is displayed.
- AC3: No record is created in the database when the email is duplicate.

Summary: List all registered dinosaurs
Description:
As an investigator, I want to see a full list of all dinosaurs registered in the system, so that I can get a general overview of all specimens in the park.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The query uses LINQ to retrieve all records from the Dinosaurs table.
- AC2: The list displays at minimum: ID, FirstName, LastName, Username, and Email for each dinosaur.
- AC3: If no dinosaurs are registered, the system displays an appropriate message.

Summary: View dinosaur detail by ID
Description:
As an investigator, I want to look up a dinosaur by its system ID, so that I can quickly access the full profile of a specific specimen.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system prompts the investigator to enter a dinosaur ID.
- AC2: The system uses LINQ to find the matching record.
- AC3: All available fields for that dinosaur are displayed.
- AC4: If no dinosaur with that ID exists, the system displays a not-found message.

Summary: View dinosaur detail by registration code
Description:
As an investigator, I want to look up a dinosaur by its lab registration code (email), so that I can retrieve specimen data using the code assigned by the laboratory.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system prompts the investigator to enter a registration code (email).
- AC2: The system uses LINQ to find the matching record.
- AC3: All available fields for that dinosaur are displayed.
- AC4: If no match is found, the system displays a not-found message.

Summary: Filter dinosaurs by zone and sector
Description:
As an investigator, I want to filter dinosaurs by their assigned zone or sector, so that I can monitor which specimens are located in a specific area of the park.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system allows filtering by Zone (equivalent to city).
- AC2: The system allows filtering by Sector (equivalent to country).
- AC3: Both filters use LINQ and return only matching records.
- AC4: The system shows the count of results returned.
- AC5: If no matches exist, an appropriate message is shown.

Summary: Filter dinosaurs by age and diet type
Description:
As an investigator, I want to filter dinosaurs by minimum age or diet type (carnivore/herbivore), so that I can study specific groups of specimens based on biological criteria.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system allows filtering dinosaurs older than a specified age using LINQ.
- AC2: The system allows filtering by diet type (carnivore or herbivore) using LINQ.
- AC3: Age input must be validated as a non-negative integer.
- AC4: Results display the relevant fields for each matching dinosaur.

Summary: Display full names and registration codes report
Description:
As an investigator, I want to generate a report showing the full name and registration code of every dinosaur, so that I can share a concise reference list with the scientific team.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The query uses a LINQ projection to return only FullName (FirstName + LastName) and Email.
- AC2: The report is displayed in a readable format.
- AC3: The report covers all registered dinosaurs.

Summary: Count dinosaurs by zone and sector
Description:
As an investigator, I want to see how many dinosaurs are assigned to each zone and each sector, so that I can assess population distribution across the park.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system uses LINQ grouping to count dinosaurs per Zone.
- AC2: The system uses LINQ grouping to count dinosaurs per Sector.
- AC3: Results display the zone/sector name and the corresponding count.
- AC4: Total count across all dinosaurs matches the general registry count.

Summary: List dinosaurs without tracking device or location
Description:
As an investigator, I want to identify dinosaurs that have no tracking device assigned or no registered location, so that I can prioritize which specimens need immediate attention.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system uses LINQ to filter dinosaurs where Phone is null or empty.
- AC2: The system uses LINQ to filter dinosaurs where Address is null or empty.
- AC3: Both lists are displayed separately with a clear label.
- AC4: If all dinosaurs have the data, the system shows an appropriate message.

Summary: List dinosaurs sorted by date and alphabetically by species
Description:
As an investigator, I want to view dinosaurs ordered by their registration date or alphabetically by species, so that I can easily navigate the registry in a structured way.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system uses LINQ OrderBy to sort by CreatedAt descending (most recent first).
- AC2: The system uses LINQ OrderBy to sort alphabetically by LastName (species).
- AC3: Both orderings are available as separate options.

Summary: Update dinosaur data fields
Description:
As an investigator, I want to modify the stored information of a registered dinosaur, so that the system always reflects the most accurate and up-to-date data for each specimen.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system allows updating any field of the Dinosaur entity (required and optional).
- AC2: After updating, the system saves the changes to the database via EF Core.
- AC3: The system displays a confirmation message once the update is successful.
- AC4: Required fields (FirstName, LastName, Username, Email) cannot be set to empty.

Summary: Update dinosaur security code with confirmation
Description:
As an investigator, I want to change the security code (password) of a dinosaur record with a confirmation step, so that accidental or unauthorized changes are prevented.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system prompts for the new security code and asks for confirmation (enter it twice).
- AC2: If both entries match, the security code is updated and a success message is shown.
- AC3: If the entries do not match, the update is rejected and an error message is displayed.
- AC4: The security code is not stored or displayed in plain text in the output.

Summary: Delete dinosaur by ID with confirmation
Description:
As an investigator, I want to remove a dinosaur from the registry by its ID after confirming the action, so that accidental deletions do not result in permanent data loss without intent.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system prompts the investigator to enter the dinosaur ID to delete.
- AC2: Before deleting, the system displays the message: "Are you sure you want to delete this dinosaur? (Y/N)".
- AC3: If confirmed (Y), the record is deleted and a success message is shown.
- AC4: If rejected (N), no changes are made.
- AC5: If the ID does not exist, an appropriate error message is displayed.

Summary: Delete dinosaur by registration code with confirmation
Description:
As an investigator, I want to remove a dinosaur from the registry by its registration code (email) after confirming the action, so that lab records can be cleared using the code assigned at registration.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: The system prompts the investigator to enter the registration code (email) to delete.
- AC2: Before deleting, the system displays: "Are you sure you want to delete this dinosaur? (Y/N)".
- AC3: If confirmed (Y), the record is deleted and a success message is shown.
- AC4: If rejected (N), no changes are made.
- AC5: If the email does not exist, an appropriate error message is displayed.

Summary: Create system diagrams
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system to be accompanied by formal documentation diagrams, so that the design and behavior of the system can be understood and reviewed by the scientific and academic team.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: A flowchart diagram covering the main system flows is created.
- AC2: A use case diagram identifying the actor (Investigator) and all system use cases is created.
- AC3: A class diagram showing the Dinosaur entity, DbContext, and their relationships is created.

Summary: Set up GitHub repository with conventional commits
Description:
As an investigator, I want the project source code hosted on GitHub with a consistent commit history, so that the development process is traceable and meets academic submission standards.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: A public GitHub repository is created for the project.
- AC2: All commits follow the conventional commits format (feat:, fix:, refactor:, docs:, chore:).
- AC3: The repository contains the full project source code organized by module.

Summary: Document system in Docusaurus
Description:
As an investigator, I want the system functionality documented in Docusaurus, so that any team member can understand how to use and maintain the system.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: A Docusaurus site is configured for the project.
- AC2: Documentation covers all modules: Insertion, Queries, Update, and Deletion.
- AC3: The site includes setup instructions (migrations, connection string, running the app).

Summary: Register activity evidence in Jira
Description:
As an investigator, I want all development activities tracked in Jira, so that the project progress is visible and auditable for academic review.

Acceptance Criteria:
- AC1: All Epics and User Stories are created in Jira before development starts.
- AC2: Each story is moved through the appropriate status columns (To Do → In Progress → Done).
- AC3: Screenshots or exports of the Jira board are included as evidence in the deliverable.
